request.question: What items could you find delivered to Trout Creek, Utah?

title: Pony Express
Trout Creek, Utah
Gauff Hill, Pennsylvania
Sheepheaven Creek redband trout
Trout River (Iowa)
Ibapah Peak
Trout Creek, Montana
Alvord cutthroat trout
Arkansas Hills
Golden trout
sentences: The Pony Express was a mail service delivering messages, newspapers, and mail.
Trout Creek is a small farming unincorporated community, in western Juab County, Utah, United States.
It is located along the Pony Express/Overland route in northern Snake Valley, north of Partoun and south of Callao.
It is named after the creek that flows from the west off of the Deep Creek Mountains.
It has one of the most remote Mormon chapels in Utah, with a short section of paved road, the only paved road for over 50 mi .
Gauff Hill is an unincorporated community in extreme eastern Salisbury Township, Lehigh County, Pennsylvania.
It is at the head of a valley between arms of South Mountain to the north and south and is separated from the Lehigh River by the former.
The East Branch Trout Creek starts just east of the village and flows west to join the South Branch in Allentown to become the Trout Creek, a tributary of the Lehigh River.
The Sheepheaven Creek redband trout is a local Californian variety of the rainbow trout ("Oncorhynchus mykiss"), a freshwater fish in the Salmonidae family.

It is considered either a distinct western form of the McCloud River redband trout (subspecies "Oncorhynchus mykiss stonei"), or a subspecies of its own, which has not been scientifically named and described yet.
It is native to Sheepheaven Creek, Siskiyou County, California, United States.
It has been transplanted into Swamp Creek in 1972 and 1974 and into Trout Creek in 1977.
They can now be found in both locations.
Sheepheaven Creek redband are found to be the most distinct among all other trout groups, and therefore has been suggested to merit recognition as a new subspecies.
They have the fewest gill rakers of any western trout.
The Trout River is a 13.5 mi tributary of the Upper Iowa River in northeastern Iowa.
It rises in Frankville Township in Winneshiek County, south of Iowa Highway 9, and flows north to join the Upper Iowa River in Glenwood Township, east of Decorah.
It is joined from the west by a major tributary, Trout Creek.
The river and creek should not be confused with another Trout Creek (and Trout Run) which flow into the Upper Iowa River 5 mi to the west at Decorah.
Ibapah Peak is a 12018 ft summit in Juab County, Utah in the United States.
It is the highest point of the Deep Creek Range and is located less than 5 mi east of the Utah-Nevada border, and about 10 mi northwest of the town of Trout Creek, Utah.
With a topographic prominence of 5247 ft it is the fifth-most prominent summit in Utah.
Trout Creek is a census-designated place (CDP) in Sanders County, Montana, United States.
The population was 261 at the 2000 census.
Trout Creek was proclaimed "Huckleberry Capital of Montana" by the state's legislature in 1981.
The Alvord cutthroat trout, "Oncorhynchus clarki alvordensis", was a subspecies of cutthroat trout.
It was known only from Trout Creek in Oregon and Virgin Creek in Nevada, although it may have lived in several of the larger Alvord Basin streams during recent times.
It was native to spring-fed creeks that ran down to Alvord Dry Lake in southeast Oregon, which was a large lake during the ice ages and an isolated drainage, part of the Great Basin today.

This is one of the two cutthroat trout taxa considered extinct because all known populations are hybridized with rainbow trout which were introduced into streams in the Alvord basin in the 1920s, resulting in cutbows.
The Arkansas Hills are a mountain range in Central Colorado located between the upper Arkansas River Valley and the upper South Platte River Valley.
This landmass is a continuation of the ridge known as the Mosquito Range north of Trout Creek Pass.
The range continues for roughly 60 miles south-southwest from Trout Creek Pass to the town of Texas Creek.
Few perennial streams flow out of the Arkansas Hills, as the area is arid.
The range is in the rain shadow of the much higher Sawatch and Sangre de Cristo Ranges to the west across the Arkansas River Valley.
The City of Salida, Colorado is the most notable municipality near the Arkansas Hills.
To the south is the town of Cotopaxi, Colorado.
To the east is the town of Guffey, Colorado.
The highest points in the Arkansas Hills tend to be between 11,000 and 12,000 feet elevation.
The California golden trout, or simply the golden trout ("Oncorhynchus mykiss aguabonita"), is a subspecies of the rainbow trout native to California.
The golden trout is native to Golden Trout Creek (tributary to the Kern River), Volcano Creek (tributary to Golden Trout Creek), and the South Fork Kern River.
It is the state fish of California.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What items could you find delivered to Trout Creek, Utah?
hop1_docs: Mammoth Creek | Mammoth Creek is a Utah creek which flows for over 20 miles through mountains and forests from Mammoth Summit (near Brian Head, Utah), through the Mammoth Valley, to its confluence with the Sevier River (near Hatch, Utah).  The creek contains wild brown trout and hatchery rainbow trout.

Trout Creek, Utah | Trout Creek is a small farming unincorporated community, in western Juab County, Utah, United States.  It is located along the Pony Express/Overland route in northern Snake Valley, north of Partoun and south of Callao.  It is named after the creek that flows from the west off of the Deep Creek Mountains.  It has one of the most remote Mormon chapels in Utah, with a short section of paved road, the only paved road for over 50 mi .

Ibapah Peak | Ibapah Peak is a 12018 ft summit in Juab County, Utah in the United States.  It is the highest point of the Deep Creek Range and is located less than 5 mi east of the Utah-Nevada border, and about 10 mi northwest of the town of Trout Creek, Utah.  With a topographic prominence of 5247 ft it is the fifth-most prominent summit in Utah.
Find What You Love and Let It Kill You | "Find What You Love and Let It Kill You" may refer to:
Find What You Love and Let It Kill You (Jonny Craig EP) | Find What You Love and Let It Kill You (Jonny Craig EP)
Letters to Judy: What Kids Wish They Could Tell You | Letters to Judy: What Kids Wish They Could Tell You

Navajo Lake (Utah) | Navajo Lake is a small lake in northwestern Kane County in southern Utah.  It is shallow, reaching depths of only 25 feet.  Recreation includes fishing, boating, swimming, and lodging facilities.  Species of trout in Navajo Lake are brook trout, splake trout (hybrid), and rainbow trout.  Trout survival for winter is very poor with only the splake and brook trout overwintering.  There is also a dense population of Utah chub.

response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Trout Creek, Utah (passage [2]): "located along the Pony Express/Overland route" ; "one of the most remote Mormon chapels" ; "only paved road for over 50 mi"
  - No passage explicitly names delivered items (no post office, mail, parcels, or delivery services mentioned).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - Ambiguous whether question asks about historical deliveries (Pony Express era) or contemporary deliveries (mail, parcels, supplies).
  - Missing explicit page or sentence that states what was/ is delivered to Trout Creek (e.g., "mail", "packages", "provisions").

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - Confirm whether Pony Express delivered mail to Trout Creek (and what exactly was carried).
  - Presence and details of any Trout Creek post office or current delivery services (USPS, courier) and types of items they deliver (letters, parcels, freight).
  - Road/access information that determines what can be delivered today (seasonal access, nearest delivery hub).

- retrieval_direction (prioritized):
  1. "Pony Express" — page describing what the Pony Express carried (to verify historical deliveries).
  2. "Trout Creek, Utah post office" or "Trout Creek Utah ZIP code" — to confirm current postal service and delivered item types.
  3. "Trout Creek, Utah (full article or county site)" — more detailed local entry for mentions of deliveries, businesses, or supply routes.
  4. "USPS deliveries to remote Utah communities" or "delivery services Juab County Utah" — to learn modern parcel/freight service options.
response.hop2_query: Trout Creek Utah post office Pony Express mail deliveries
hop2_docs: Trout Creek, Utah | Trout Creek is a small farming unincorporated community, in western Juab County, Utah, United States.  It is located along the Pony Express/Overland route in northern Snake Valley, north of Partoun and south of Callao.  It is named after the creek that flows from the west off of the Deep Creek Mountains.  It has one of the most remote Mormon chapels in Utah, with a short section of paved road, the only paved road for over 50 mi .

U.S. Special Delivery (postal service) | U.S. Special Delivery was a postal service paid for with additional postage for urgent letters and postal packets which are delivered in less time than by standard or first class mail service.  Its meaning is different and separate from express mail delivery service.  Essentially it meant that a postal packet was delivered from a post office to the addressee immediately once it arrived at the post office responsible for delivering it, rather than waiting for the next regular delivery to the addressee.

Discontinued post office | A discontinued post office or DPO is an American postal term for a post office which has been discontinued.  Some are in ghost towns, some victims of consolidation of mail service as small post offices are closed or a city expands.  The introduction of Rural Free Delivery, RFD, in 1902 led to the closure of many post offices, which peaked in 1901 at 76,945.  In the United States, which was mostly rural, mail previously had been picked up in rural areas at small local post offices, home delivery being limited to urban areas until experimentation with rural delivery began in 1890.
Pony Express | The Pony Express was a mail service delivering messages, newspapers, and mail.

Gandy, Utah | Gandy is a small farming unincorporated community in Millard County, Utah, United States, located just east of the Nevada border.  It is located in the west-central part of Snake Valley.  It is known for Gandy Warm Springs and Gandy Creek, a large spring (15-19 cfs) that comes out of the base of Spring Mountain to the west.  It stays around 81–82 degrees Fahrenheit (27–28 Celsius) year-round.  Originally known as Smithville, Gandy was renamed in 1925 after Isaac Gandy, the first ranch owner in the area back when this was a post office stop along the Pony Express/Overland Route.

Express mail | Express mail is an accelerated mail delivery service for which the customer pays a surcharge and receives faster delivery.  Express mail is a service for domestic and international mail and is in most countries governed by a country's own postal administration.  Since 1998, the international accelerated delivery services are governed by the EMS Cooperative.

Rural Free Delivery | Rural Free Delivery (RFD) is a service which began in the United States in the late 19th century, to deliver mail directly to rural farm families.  Prior to RFD, individuals living in more remote homesteads had to pick up mail themselves at sometimes distant post offices or pay private carriers for delivery.  RFD became a political football, with politicians promising it to voters and using it themselves to reach voters.  The proposal to offer free rural delivery was not universally embraced.  Private carriers and local shopkeepers feared a loss of business.  The United States Post Office Department began experiments with Rural Free Delivery as early as 1890.  However, it was not until 1893, when Georgia Congressman Thomas E. Watson pushed through legislation, that the practice was mandated.  However, universal implementation was slow; RFD was not adopted generally in the United States Post Office until 1902.  The rural delivery service uses a network of rural routes traveled by carriers to deliver and pick up mail to and from roadside mailboxes.
response.summary_2: Mail — specifically messages and newspapers carried by the Pony Express. Trout Creek lies along the Pony Express/Overland route, and the Pony Express delivered messages, newspapers, and mail.
